command.text: awk 'NR==1{print;next} NR==FNR{a[$1]=$0;next} $1 in a{print a[$1],$0;delete a[$1]} END{for(i in a)print a[i]}' /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt /testbed/dir2/subdir2/textfile5.txt

raw_output_payload.reasoning: We need a bash command that outputs a table merging lines where first field matches. Keep the line that starts with "Gene" at start. Likely use awk or join. Assuming files have same delimiter? Use join -t? Let's assume whitespace. Use join -1 1 -2 1. But need to keep Gene line. So cat file1 file2? Actually merging lines: join -t ' ' -1 1 -2 1 file1 file2. Then prepend Gene line. Use awk to print. Command: (awk 'NR==1{print;next} {print}' /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt | join -1 1 -2 1 - /testbed/dir2/subdir2/textfile5.txt). But join requires sorted. Maybe use awk to merge. Simpler: paste? But merging by first field. Use awk to build associative arrays. Let's produce: awk 'NR==FNR{a[$1]=$0;next} $1 in a{print a[$1],$0;delete a[$1]} END{for(i in a)print a[i]}' file1 file2. Then prepend Gene line: awk 'NR==1{print;next} ...' but Gene line is first line of file1? So we can just output Gene line first. So final: awk 'NR==1{print;next} NR==FNR{a[$1]=$0;next} $1 in a{print a[$1],$0;delete a[$1]} END{for(i in a)print a[i]}' /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt /testbed/dir2/subdir2/textfile5.txt.

task.query: Create a table containing all information from /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt and /testbed/dir2/subdir2/textfile5.txt, merging lines where the first field of both files matches, and keeping the line that starts with "Gene" at the start of the file.
task.gold: join -a1 -a2 <(sed s/^Gene/00ne/ /testbed/dir1/subdir1/textfile4.txt | sort) <(sed s/^Gene/00ne/ /testbed/dir2/subdir2/textfile5.txt | sort) | column -t | sed s/^00ne/Gene/
